1. Builds the pot when I have a strong hand
2. Takes advantage of position - I'll act last on every post-flop street
3. Isolates the blinds with good fold equity
4. Is a standard, non-exploitable sizing that's consistent with my overall strategy

The current pot is 150 (50+100), and with my 300 raise, the effective pot odds for my opponents to call are reasonable but not attractive. Flash (SB) needs to call 250 more with 7975 chips, and Elon (BB) needs to call 200 more with 22570 chips.

ATs plays well post-flop from the button, and I want to build value while maintaining my aggressive, high-roller image at this tournament stage (Hand 112 of 150).

Holding Ten-Nine suited in the Small Blind against a Button open is a standard calling hand in a 3-handed game. It has great post-flop playability and equity. While 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while playing out of position for the rest of the hand.

3d7s is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low cards, and large gaps. In the big blind facing a button raise to 300 and SB call, the opponents' ranges are decently strong. Pot odds of ~3.5:1 are not sufficient for this speculative hand with minimal equity against their ranges (~25-30% at best). Deep stack allows folding trash to avoid difficult postflop spots and preserve chips for better opportunities in late tournament.

On a monotone flop of 2c-6c-3c, I have absolutely no connection with the board. I hold 9d-Td, which has no clubs and no current pair or meaningful draw. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the best course of action is to check and likely fold to any significant aggression, as I have zero equity on this texture.

Betting 450 chips on the flop. My As-Ts has the best hand with Ace-high on a 2c-6c-3c board after Flash checked. This bet (64% of current pot) builds value while maintaining control. Flash likely whiffed or has a weaker holding. Even if behind, I have 6+ outs with overcard possibilities. This sizing is aggressive but not reckless given the strength of my hand and position.

On this monotone club flop, I have absolutely no equity. I have no clubs in my hand, no pairs, and no straight draws. Claude was the pre-flop aggressor and is following through with a bet. There is no reason to continue with Ten-high air out of position on this texture.

I am on the button with 9-7 offsuit, which is a marginal hand even in three-handed play. While I have been aggressive in recent hands, opening with 9-7o against a massive stack in the small blind (Elon) and an increasingly active big blind (Claude) is risky. Given my current stack of 77 big blinds, I don't need to force a steal here. I'll fold this hand to preserve my stack and look for a more favorable spot to apply pressure.

Preflop in small blind vs big blind after button folds. Hand is Kc4h offsuit, which is weak and likely dominated by any calling range from Claude. Facing only 50 to call into 150 pot offers odds, but playing out of position postflop with such a marginal hand risks losing more chips on unfavorable boards. Standard play is to fold trash like K4o from SB to preserve stack and avoid difficult spots OOP. Deep stacks don't change this; better to wait for stronger holdings.

On the button in a 3-handed pot with Jc3c, a suited gapped connector with flush and straight potential. Deep effective stacks (opponents ~7k-9k, me 22k), position allows wide raising range to steal blinds or isolate. Standard open-raise size 3x BB to 300 builds pot while keeping aggression high early in tournament. Opponents have folded frequently to pre-flop raises in history. Folding too tight, calling risks multiway without initiative.

Hand #114: I hold 8♣10♠ in the SB against Elon's button raise to 300. While this is a marginal hand, the pot odds (450 to call 250, approximately 1.8:1) make it worthwhile to see a flop with a suited connector. Elon's raise from the button is likely a steal attempt given typical button ranges, and I'm not in desperate chip territory at 9,705 chips. If Flash folds, I'll have decent position postflop to apply pressure on Elon. The hand has potential to make straights and flushes. Calling is more balanced than folding here and allows me to realize equity. I'm at hand 114/150, so preservation of chips while maintaining flexibility is important.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Four offsuit. The Button (Elon) has raised to 3BB and the Small Blind (Claude) has called. While I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 700), Q4o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. I would be first to act on all subsequent streets, and the risk of being dominated or losing more chips on later streets outweighs the potential reward of flopping a miracle. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Checking with 8♣10♠ on a K♣2♥T♦ flop is the correct play. I have only a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to make the nuts with a 9) and no current made hand. Elon has position and likely has stronger holdings that connect with this board. By checking, I either get a free card if he checks back, or I can make an informed pot odds decision if he bets. Leading into the aggressor with a weak draw would commit unnecessary chips from my 9,455 stack in a tournament situation where chip preservation is important. This is hand 114 of 150, and I should avoid bleeding chips with marginal holdings.

As the pre-flop raiser on the button with position, facing a check from Claude on a dry Kc-2h-Td flop (king-high, disconnected, minimal draws). My Jc3c suited connector has no pair but includes a backdoor club flush draw (one club on board) and backdoor straight potential. The board favors my wide raising range, which includes Kx, Tx, overpairs, and bluffs. Claude's pre-flop call from SB and check-flop suggest medium strength or draws, but he folds to aggression often in history (e.g., multiple folds to c-bets). Pot 700, standard half-pot c-bet of 400 applies pressure, builds pot when ahead, denies equity, and takes it down ~60-70% of time profitably given fold equity. Deep stacks allow this; check risks giving free cards to any draws. Optimal bluff spot to maximize EV.

With a made hand (pair of tens), pot odds of 3.75:1, and a moderate stack at hand 114/150, calling is justified. I need roughly 27% equity to breakeven and have a reasonable showdown hand against Elon's likely c-bet range. The K on the board is concerning, but middle pair with decent pot odds warrants seeing another card. I'm getting fair compensation for my out-of-position situation.

I'm holding 8♣10♠ on a K♣2♥T♦T♣ board with a made hand (pair of tens). Elon checked to me on the turn, which suggests weakness or hesitation. He raised to 400 on the flop with the original K-2-T board, but the second ten may have given him pause—especially if he holds a king that now plays poorly against trips/two-pair possibilities, or if he's on a draw. My pair of tens is likely ahead of many holdings in his range. With a pot of 1,500 and a stack of 9,055, betting 700 is a balanced play that: (1) extracts value from weaker king holdings, (2) applies pressure on draws, and (3) doesn't overcommit me to a single street. The bet size is proportional to pot and stack, consistent with sound tournament poker at hand 114/150. If called, I'll have value information for the river.

Facing a check-raise on the turn after calling my flop c-bet with Jc3c, a complete air hand on Kc2hTdTc board. Paired tens favor Claude's range including KT, TT, sets like KK/TT/22, or strong draws he turned into value. Pre-flop SB call and flop call suggest medium strength that improved or trapping. No pair, jack-high no showdown, backdoor club draw now 4-flush but board has two clubs, minimal equity (~10-20% vs range). Pot 2200, calling 700 (1/3 pot) poor EV without fold equity or draws. Raising escalates with 21k stack vs his 8k but dominated. Deep tournament, fold preserves stack for 36 hands left, exploits future spots vs Claude who folds often but shows strength here. Standard fold to check-raise without equity.

2-Jack offsuit is a trash hand with very limited playability. While I'm in late position (button) which is normally favorable for stealing blinds, this hand doesn't have sufficient equity to warrant a raise or even a call. At 35 hands remaining in the tournament, I should be selective with my entries and preserve chips for stronger situations. With moderate stacks around the table and no ICM pressure yet, folding weak hands and waiting for premium holdings is the mathematically correct long-term strategy. This is a straightforward fold.

In a 3-handed game, after the Button (Claude) folds, I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. This is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and exert pressure on the Big Blind (Elon), rather than just completing the blind and playing a limp pot out of position.

3-handed with button folded, facing standard SB min-raise from aggressive player Flash who opens wide from SB (~40-50% range). K7o blocks strong king hands like AK/KQ and has decent equity (~38-40% vs wide range). BB position postflop (acts last), pot odds excellent (200 into 400, 3:1 direct + implied odds with deep stacks and my huge chip lead allowing playability). Folding would be too tight; calling defends wide range profitably. No need to 3-bet bluff here as hand has showdown value and postflop playability.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-versus-blind battle. The flop of K-7-3 rainbow is very dry and favors the range of the pre-flop raiser, as I am more likely to hold strong Kings (AK, KQ, KJ) than the Big Blind. I will place a standard continuation bet of half the pot to put pressure on Elon's non-paired hands and take control of the pot immediately.

Flop hits my K7o perfectly for top two pair (kings and sevens) on a dry K-7-3 rainbow board, crushing Flash's wide SB opening range which includes many Ax, worse kings (KJ/KT/K9), bluffs, and weaker pairs. His 1/3 pot c-bet is standard continuation but weak; we have a monster disguised as a blocking hand preflop. Raising for thin value and protection against any live draws (e.g., QJ, 54s) or floats. Sizing to 1200 total (~4x his bet, 40% pot) balances value from calls with weaker hands while keeping pot manageable against villain's shorter stack (7025) and my massive chip lead. Calling is too passive given strength and position; folding impossible. Avoid over-raising to prevent folds from marginal hands we dominate.

On a very dry board like K-7-3 rainbow, Elon's 4x raise (from 300 to 1200) is highly polarizing. He is either representing a very strong hand (like a King, 77, or 33) or he is attempting a bluff because the board is so disconnected. While my Ace-high has some showdown value in a blind-versus-blind battle, calling a large raise out of position with no draw and only one overcard to the King is statistically unfavorable. If I call and the turn is a blank, I am forced to fold to any further aggression. The most disciplined play here is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Q7o. While opening ranges are wider in 3-handed play, Q7o is a marginal hand that doesn't play well post-flop, especially against active opponents like Elon and Claude who both have larger stacks. Given the current tournament stage (Hand 116 of 150) and my position as the shortest stack, I prefer to wait for a stronger hand rather than risking chips on a steal attempt that is likely to be contested.

In the small blind facing the big blind with 6s Tc offsuit, a weak hand with poor connectivity, low high-card strength, and offsuit nature limiting flush/straight potential. Preflop SB vs BB is a stealing spot due to fold equity, but Claude's history shows he defends wide and calls stations in BB spots. Raising commits chips OOP with marginal postflop playability; limping induces multiway pots or traps value. Folding preserves our deep stack (223 BBs) for premium hands in this late tournament stage (hand 116/150), avoiding speculative equity realization OOP. Optimal to wait for stronger holdings.

3-handed play on the button with T2s, a suited gapped connector with good playability. Excellent position to steal the 150 in the pot (likely blinds posted, SB limped or small action leading to to-call 100). Opponents have shown passive tendencies in spots but also aggression; however, wide raising range from button maximizes EV chip-wise in late tournament. Raising standard 3x BB to 300 applies pressure, builds pot with position if called, and T2s has decent equity multiway or heads-up. Folding too tight, calling speculative but raise best. Minraise too small, pot commit low.

With 9dJd in the small blind, I'm facing an out-of-position decision with a marginal hand. While J9 has some potential, it's a weak-medium holding that plays poorly in 3-way pots and especially out of position. The big blind Flash hasn't yet acted and could re-raise, which would put me in an even worse spot. My stack (10555) is healthy enough to be selective with marginal hands. There's no reason to commit chips with J9 against Elon's button raise when I'll be out of position post-flop. I can find better opportunities with this stack in later positions or with stronger holdings.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Threes. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more to see a flop. Pocket Threes are a solid hand for set-mining, and with an effective stack of nearly 70 big blinds, I have sufficient implied odds to call and try to hit a set on the flop. Re-raising (3-betting) is an option, but playing a small pair out of position against the chip leader is often less ideal than taking a flop and evaluating.

I am holding pocket Threes on a board of Qd-5h-5s. This is a paired board, which is generally good for the pre-flop caller when the pre-flop raiser (Elon) misses, but it's also a board where the raiser will frequently continuation bet. My Threes are a marginal made hand here. I will check to the aggressor to see how he chooses to proceed. Leading out into the button raiser on this board doesn't accomplish much, as I'll only get called by better hands (Queens, better pocket pairs, or a Five) and fold out hands I currently beat. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially catch a bluff.

Flop is Qd 5h 5s, paired fives with a queen. I raised pre-flop from button with Ts2s suited, Flash called from BB. Flash checks first in, giving me position heads-up. My hand: flush draw (two spades + 5s on board, ~9 flush outs for 35-40% equity), ten-high no pair. Paired board caps strong hands somewhat, Flash's check suggests weakness or trapping, but history shows Flash passive post-flop often folding to c-bets. Deep stack (22k effective vs his 6.7k), late tournament. Best play: continuation bet as semi-bluff to leverage equity, position, and fold equity. Target 2/3 pot (~430) for pressure without overbetting; sizing 400 builds pot while denying equity. Check risks giving free card to villain's draws/overs; betting maximizes EV.

On a paired board of Qd-5h-5s, my pocket Threes are currently a marginal made hand. Elon's continuation bet of 400 into a pot of approximately 650 is a standard move for a pre-flop aggressor on such a dry board. While I am behind any Queen, any Five, and any pocket pair from 44 to AA, I am ahead of his pure air and overcard hands (like AK, AJ, etc.). Folding immediately with a pocket pair on this texture would be too weak and exploitable against an aggressive player like Elon. I will call the flop bet to see the turn and evaluate his further aggression; if he continues to barrel on the turn, especially on an overcard, I can reconsider.

On this dry, paired board (Qd-5h-5s-6c), my pocket Threes are a very weak made hand. I called the flop continuation bet to keep Elon's bluffs in and see if he would slow down on the turn. Since I am out of position, I will continue to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out here doesn't represent much and would likely only get called or raised by better hands (a Queen, a Five, or a higher pocket pair). By checking, I can see how Elon chooses to proceed. If he checks back, I might have the best hand and can potentially see a cheap river. If he bets again, I will have to carefully evaluate the bet size, but 33 is likely too weak to withstand a second barrel.

Heads-up on turn with Ts2s on Qd5h5s6c board. Pot 1450, Flash checked (effective stack ~6300). Strong spade flush draw (9 outs, ~19% to river flush), backdoor straight potential. Preflop raise and flop c-bet continuation as semi-bluff holds initiative and position. Flash check-call flop on paired board suggests capped range: weak Qx, lower pairs (44-22, 77-AA unlikely to check-call thinly), draws (spades/hearts, 47 st draw), floats. Check-call then turn check indicates weakness, unlikely monster. Betting semi-bluffs for value from worse draws/weak pairs, fold equity (history shows Flash folds to aggression post-flop), denies equity to villain's draws (e.g., Qx flush draw, OESD). Sizing 1000 (~2/3 pot) balances pressure, builds to ~2450 pot for river jam if check-raised or hit; larger risks dead money if villain shoves wide, smaller too station-callable. Checking realizes equity but misses fold equity/value, risks villain pairing outs. Late tournament, deep vs short stack: chip EV favors aggression exploiting passive tendencies.

On the turn, the board is Qd-5h-5s-6c. My pocket Threes remain a very weak made hand, only beating 22 or pure bluffs that haven't even paired the board. Elon, the chip leader and pre-flop aggressor, has followed up his flop continuation bet with a substantial 1,000 chip bet into a 1,450 pot. This 'double barrel' on such a dry board suggests either a strong hand (a Queen, a Five, or a high pocket pair) or a determined bluff. Given that I have almost no potential to improve (only two Threes remain in the deck) and I am out of position, calling here would likely lead to an even tougher decision on the river. My Threes are effectively a bluff-catcher with very low equity against his overall range. It is more prudent to fold now and preserve my stack for a better spot.

7-6 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't warrant entry even on the button with 1.5:1 odds. With a healthy 70+ big blind stack and 32 hands remaining, I should fold marginal hands and wait for premium opportunities. The hand lacks the equity and playability needed to profitably navigate postflop situations against two opponents.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 5s, a decent suited connector. It has been folded to me. In SB vs BB play, I want to take the initiative rather than just completing the blind, especially since Elon (the Big Blind) has been playing very aggressively as the chip leader. Raising to 300 has been my consistent open-sizing throughout the tournament and I will maintain that here to represent a strong range and attempt to win the blinds or play the hand with the lead.

Pocket Queens is one of the best hands in poker, especially pre-flop. Facing a standard raise from the Small Blind (Flash, who has been aggressive in prior hands), the play is to 3-bet for value and to isolate, as the Button has folded. Flash's range is likely wide from SB, and QQ crushes it. A 3-bet to 900 (3x the raise) builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against marginal hands. My large stack allows pressure without overcommitting effectively (Flash has ~6k). Calling is too passive and invites multi-way pots or sets. Folding is never an option.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 5s and opened for 300, only to be met with a 3bet to 900 from Elon in the Big Blind. While 7s 5s is a playable hand in many situations, it performs poorly against a 3bet from the chip leader who has position on me for the rest of the hand. Calling 600 more out of position with a weak suited gapper is a losing play long-term, and 4-betting as a bluff is too risky given Elon's stack size and aggression. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 2-7 offsuit, which is the worst starting hand in poker. As the first to act on the button, there is no strategic value in playing this hand, especially with a stack of roughly 60 big blinds and two healthy stacks in the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In the small blind with 2s9h, a trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Flash on the button has folded, leaving us heads-up against Claude in the BB. While SB defends wide against BB steals, 29o has terrible equity, playability, and fold equity if raising. Calling 50 into 200 pot gives poor implied odds even if we hit. Standard play is to fold and preserve the stack for better spots in this deep-stacked 3-handed tournament with 31 hands left.

3-handed poker on the button, first to act pre-flop after SB fold (inferred from pot 150 and to-call 100). Facing BB only. K3o is a profitable open-raise from button vs BB due to position, king blocker reducing villain's Kx, and wide stealing range in short-handed play. Standard raise size 3x BB to 300 to build pot and take initiative. Deep effective stacks (BB has ~60BB), but position allows folding to strong 3bet if needed. Maximizes EV by stealing blinds frequently late in tournament.

K8o from the small blind facing a button raise is a fold. The hand is marginal with a weak kicker, I'll be out of position post-flop against an aggressive opponent with a stack advantage, and my equity (~32-35%) doesn't justify calling. Discipline in marginal spots is crucial late in the tournament with a healthy stack. I'll preserve chips for better opportunities.

In a 3-handed game, Jack-Ten offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a button raise from Elon. The pot odds are favorable, requiring about 31% equity to make the call. Since Elon's button opening range is likely wide and I have a playable hand that can hit many flops, calling is the most standard and cost-effective play to see a flop without over-committing against the chip leader.

I have flopped top pair with a decent kicker (Ten) in a 3-handed game. Against a button opener, this hand is very strong. I will follow the standard procedure of checking to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to see how he evaluates the board and potentially catch him bluffing with a wide range of hands that missed. If he bets, I plan on calling to keep the pot manageable while I have a strong but non-nutted hand.

Flop board 5c Jc 9s is draw-heavy with flush and straight draw possibilities (e.g., clubs, T8, 78). As preflop raiser from button with position, my wide range (Kx, broadway, suited connectors) hits this board decently often with top pair (KJ, J9, KQ), overpairs, or draws. Kh blocks strong KQ/KJ combos. K3o is bluff-catcher but primarily a semi-bluff/c-bet spot to deny equity to villain's calling range (pairs below J, suited connectors, Ax suited). Flash check-folds weak hands often in history (e.g., Hands 16,45,81), and his BB calling range vs button raise is capped without premium. Deep stacks but Flash has ~57BB effective; c-bet 2/3 pot (~450) balances value (KJ, sets) and bluffs, takes initiative, builds pot if called. Folding or checking weak here misses dead money and lets villain realize equity cheaply. Late tournament (120/150), aggression maximizes EV by forcing folds from marginal hands.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack and a Ten kicker. In a 3-handed game, this is a very strong hand, especially against a button open from Elon, who has been active and likely has a wide range. The flop contains both a flush draw and some straight draws (87, QT, T8). I have the Ten of Clubs, which provides a slight blocker to some of those draws. Elon's bet of 450 into 650 is a standard continuation bet. Calling is the best play here; it allows me to keep the pot manageable and keeps Elon's bluffs in his range. Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me (AJ, KJ, or sets) and would force out the weaker hands I currently dominate.

On the turn, the 7 of clubs is a significant card as it completes both a potential flush and several straights (T8, 86). I have top pair (Jacks) and now have the Ten of clubs, giving me a flush draw. Being out of position against the chip leader, the most prudent move is to check. This allows me to see if Elon continues his aggression on a board that significantly hits a defender's range. If he checks back, I am likely ahead. If he bets, I have enough equity with top pair and the flush draw to continue, but I do not want to lead into him and risk a raise on such a coordinated board.

Turn board 5c Jc 9s 7c is extremely wet with completed club flush, open-enders (T8), and pair + draw combos. Kh3c blocks strong KQ/KJ, gives king-high bluff-catcher value, and crucially holds the 3c for live nut flush draw (9 outs to flush, ~18% from flush alone, plus ~3 overcard outs to pair for ~12 total outs or 48% equity heads-up if called). Flash's BB check-call flop range (defending wide vs button open: Jx/9x/5x, AcXc, suited connectors like 67c/T8, weak Ax) gets capped without sets/two-pair; many missed draws or medium strength now facing completed board. His turn check indicates caution (no nutted hand traps aggressively), ripe for fold equity. Position + initiative as preflop raiser justifies semi-bluff bet sizing ~60% pot (900 into 1550) to: maximize fold equity from ~60% of his range (pairs below J, busted draws); deny equity to remaining draws; build pot for river shoves if called by worse (inferior draws/pairs). Flash's history shows frequent check-fold to aggression (e.g., Hands 16,45,81,111); his 52BB stack vulnerable to pressure late (hand 120/150, 3-handed). Checking concedes initiative, risks free river card realizing villain equity or check-raise, suboptimal vs short-handed passivity. Bet balances value (if villain weak) and bluffs, +EV long-term stack growth.

On the turn, the 7 of clubs is a highly dynamic card. It completes flushes and some straights (like T8). However, my hand has improved significantly; I still have top pair (Jacks), and I have picked up a flush draw with the Ten of clubs. I also have a gutshot to the straight. Elon's bet of 900 into a pot of 1550 is a strong continuation, but given the strength of my hand and the additional equity from the flush draw, folding is out of the question. Calling is preferable to raising here because it keeps Elon's bluffs and weaker semi-bluffs in his range, and it prevents me from bloating the pot and potentially getting shoved on by a made flush or straight while I am still drawing to a better hand. By calling, I maintain a manageable stack relative to the pot for the river.

On the river, the board is 5c-Jc-9s-7c-8s. I hold the Jack of Hearts and the Ten of Clubs, which gives me a Jack-high straight (7-8-9-10-J). However, this board is extremely coordinated and dangerous. The 7c on the turn completed a possible club flush, and the 8s on the river completes several other potential straights, including a higher straight if Elon holds a Queen and a Ten (8-9-10-J-Q) or a lower straight if he holds a 6 (5-6-7-8-9). Elon has been the aggressor throughout the hand, betting the flop and turn. By checking, I control the pot size and allow Elon to potentially check back with medium-strength hands that I beat (like a set or two pair) or bluff with hands that missed. If I bet, I risk being raised by a flush or a superior straight, putting me in a difficult position with a significant portion of my stack already in the pot. Therefore, checking is the most strategic move to navigate this complex board out of position.

River 8s bricks my 3c flush draw completely, leaving pure king-high on a board 5c-Jc-9s-7c-8s with club flushes possible, pair + SD combos, and minor spade fillers irrelevant. Villain check-called flop (450) and turn (900) as BB defender vs my button aggression, capping his range without monsters pre-call but strengthening to likely Jx (JT-J9), 97s/J7s two-pair, sets (JJ/99/77/55 rare), or stubborn flush draws now bricked. His river check disfavors nuts (flushes/sets would often bet for value or check-raise) and suggests medium strength showdown value or total air (unlikely after two calls). King-high beats pure air/bluffs (minimal in range) but loses to all pairs/SD hits; thin value unlikely as villain snaps top pair. Bluffing now poor - villain invested 1350+dead money, pot-committal to call small, folds only worse (rare), calls betters. Checking realizes EV: pots money (3350, +3:1 implied already), free showdown vs potential worse (Ax clubs missed, weak Jx), avoids variance/risk to massive stack (21k vs his 4.4k remaining, late 120/150 3-handed). History: Flash passive post-oop calls (Hand2 river call, Hand6 river bet/ fold), check-folds aggression (16,45,81), but paid off here indicating strength - don't overbluff sticky BB. Check preserves stacks for endgame, +EV long-term.

2-7 offsuit is the lowest-ranking hand in poker with virtually no value. Despite having position on the button, the fundamental weakness of this hand makes folding the correct play. My stack (10,555) is healthy and I can wait for better opportunities. Even in 3-handed play where button position is valuable, I need actual cards to justify a play.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 6d. While it's a blind-vs-blind situation after Claude's fold, 96o is a very weak hand to play out of position against the chip leader, Elon, who has shown plenty of aggression throughout the tournament. With over 75 big blinds, there is no need to force a play with a sub-marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jd 5h. While J5 offsuit is a weak hand, it's a 3-handed game and I am in the best position. However, looking at the recent history, both Elon and Claude have been playing aggressively and 3-betting or raising frequently. J5o doesn't play well against a raise or even in a multi-way pot. Folding here is a disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity, especially since I am the shortest stack at the table.

Preflop in the small blind with Qs4h, a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and playability. Flash has folded from the button, leaving heads-up vs Claude in the BB. Calling 50 into a 150 pot offers 3:1 odds, but Q4o has low equity against a wide BB calling range (~35-45% equity) and is difficult to play post-flop without position advantage outweighing it. Raising has no fold equity as BB gets dead money and can call/3bet wide; minraise to 200 is too small for value or semi-bluff. Standard fold to preserve stack and avoid speculative spots in a tournament with deep stacks.

On the button with 89o in an unopened pot (just blinds posted, to call 100). Position is excellent, hand has decent playability postflop with straight potential. Standard play is to raise for fold equity against blinds and to build pot when called. Opponents have folded to steals before; deep stacks but tournament pressure near end (hand 123/150). Raising 3x BB to 300 total is balanced. Call is too passive; fold too tight.

Folding 4-2o from the small blind against Elon's raise. This is the worst starting hand in poker with no showdown value. Being out of position and holding trash, calling 250 more would be poor value. Preserving my stack (10,555) for stronger opportunities is the correct strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with Q4 offsuit. Elon, the chip leader, raised from the button to 300. While the price to call is only 200 chips more, Q4o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a stack that can put significant pressure on me post-flop. With 75 big blinds, I have plenty of room to wait for better spots and don't need to defend with such a marginal holding.

3-8 offsuit is the worst hand in poker (literally called 'the curse'). While I have positional advantage on the button, this holding has minimal value both pre-flop and post-flop. Even though the pot odds are reasonable at 2.5:1 to call, my hand has virtually no equity against the likely ranges of the blinds, and I'll be playing out of position post-flop in multiway pots when behind most holdings. Given my stack size (10,555) and the quality of hands I've been playing throughout the tournament, folding marginal hands and preserving chips for premium spots is the correct long-term strategy. Flash and Elon both have active stacks, and I should wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 5s, which is a very weak hand. The action has folded to me. While I could attempt a steal or complete the bet, the Big Blind (Elon) is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively. Playing a marginal hand like 6-5 offsuit out of position against a large stack is not ideal. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 75-big-blind stack.

I have been dealt Ace-Queen suited on the button, which is a premium hand in any format and exceptionally strong in 3-handed play. My goal here is to open for value and take control of the pot with position. I will stick to my established opening size of 300 chips.

Pocket 44 is a strong hand against a button raise range, which is likely wide given Flash's frequent aggression from the history. Facing a min-raise equivalent from the button, 3-betting isolates, builds the pot for value, and takes initiative OOP. Folding is too tight; flat-calling leads to difficult OOP spots postflop with implied odds that are decent but suboptimal multiway. Raise to 900 (3x the raise size) is a standard sizing to pressure while keeping his calling range beatable. If he shoves (possible given shortish stack of ~72BB), 44 has good equity vs his wide shoving range.

This is a straightforward fold. I'm holding 6-2 offsuit, one of the worst hands in poker, in the big blind facing a 3-bet. Elon's re-raise to 900 indicates significant strength, and Flash's initial raise shows they have a playable hand. With 62o, I have minimal equity against any reasonable range in this scenario, and the position (big blind) makes post-flop play difficult. My stack of 10455 is healthy enough that I can afford to wait for better spots rather than bleed chips with trash hands. Folding is the correct mathematical and strategic decision here.

I have been dealt Ace-Queen of Diamonds on the button, which is a premium hand in 3-handed play. Elon (Small Blind) has shown significant aggression recently, frequently 3-betting and putting pressure on my opening raises. My initial open was to 300, and Elon's 3-bet to 900 is a standard sizing. While calling to play in position is a viable strategy, I want to 4-bet here for value and to discourage him from constantly attacking my button opens. A 4-bet to 2200 chips represents a strong hand and puts the pressure back on him. If he shoves, I am likely committed to calling given the hand strength and the shorthanded dynamics.

Facing Flash's large 4-bet to 2200 (over 1.5x the pot) on the button after my 3-bet. Although Flash is frequently aggressive from the button, this sizing polarizes his range towards premium hands like QQ+, AK suited/off, possibly JJ/TT occasionally. Pocket 44 is dominated by this range (~18% equity vs QQ+/AK) and set-mining OOP with his remaining 5325 stack limits implied odds (SPR ~1.4 after call). Postflop play would be difficult OOP with vulnerable low set potential. Folding is GTO-correct here to preserve our deep 210bb stack for superior spots late in the tournament with 25 hands left and 3 players.

Preflop on the button, first to act in a 3-handed spot with deep stacks. 5c8d is a trash hand: offsuit, low, gapped connectors with poor playability. No steal value here against competent opponents like Flash and Claude who defend blinds frequently based on history. Standard play is to fold weak hands and wait for better spots to apply pressure. Raising or limping risks playing OOP postflop with a weak range. Goal is long-term stack maximization, not speculative plays.

Heads-up defense from small blind with Q-3. Excellent pot odds (3:1) for a cheap call. In heads-up play, Q-high has reasonable equity against a wide BB range. Position advantage post-flop makes this a profitable long-term play. The call is only 50 chips with room to play the hand after seeing the flop.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand (8c 2d). Claude limped in from the small blind after Elon folded. There is no reason to build a pot with such a weak hand, so I will check and take the free flop.

Checking is the prudent play with a marginal hand (pair of threes). The flop is relatively dry, and Flash's pre-flop check suggests a weaker holding. By checking, I gather information about Flash's actual strength without committing chips to the pot unnecessarily. This allows me to make better decisions on the turn based on Flash's action. If Flash bets, I can reassess; if Flash checks, I maintain flexibility with showdown possibilities or river decisions.

On the flop of 8s-3d-4h, I have top pair with an Eight. In a blind-versus-blind limped pot, this is a relatively strong hand. Claude checked to me, which often signifies a weak range or a desire to see a cheap turn. I will bet 100 (half the pot) to extract value from smaller pairs (3x, 4x), straight draws like 56 or 67, and to protect my hand against overcards like King-high or Ace-high which have significant equity against me.

Calling Flash's small flop bet with a pair of 3s and Queen kicker. The 100-chip raise into a 300-chip pot appears to be a probe bet or weak holding, suggesting Flash is uncertain about their hand strength. I'm getting 4:1 odds and have reasonable equity with my pair and two overcards in the deck. A call preserves my flexibility while keeping the hand alive. Against Flash's apparent weakness, this is a profitable defensive play that allows me to reassess on the turn based on their subsequent action.

The turn 7s is a blank that doesn't improve my pair of 3s. Flash's check on the turn suggests they either have a weak holding, a missed draw, or are playing passively. With a marginal hand (pair of 3s with Queen kicker), betting here is too aggressive and likely unprofitable. Checking allows me to reach showdown cheaply, evaluate Flash's river action with perfect information, and avoid overcommitting chips to what is likely a losing hand. The small blind position gives me information advantage post-river if Flash acts first. Preserving chips with a weak hand while maintaining flexibility to fold to a strong river bet is the optimal approach.

I have top pair with an Eight, but my kicker is a Deuce, which is extremely weak. After Claude checked the turn, checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my showdown equity without over-committing to a hand that could be easily out-kicked or beaten by a turned straight (like 56). I've already extracted one street of value on the flop; checking now is a safe way to reach the river.

Checking is the optimal play with a weak hand (pair of 3s) against an opponent who has demonstrated consistent weakness through their turn check. This preserves my remaining chips while maintaining showdown value. If Flash checks behind, I win the hand. If Flash bets, I have the flexibility to fold knowing my hand is unlikely best. The marginal strength of my holding and Flash's clear weakness pattern make this a chip-preservation play that still allows me to win the pot without additional investment.

I have a pair of eights on a board of 8s-3d-4h-7s-5h. While I have top pair, my kicker (Deuce) is the weakest possible. The river 5h completes a straight for any hand containing a Six (4-5-6-7-8). Claude has check-called the flop and checked both the turn and the river. In a limped pot, his range is very wide. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands (maybe a 7 or a 5, but even those are doubtful) and will almost certainly be beat by any other 8 or a straight if he calls or raises. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown equity and potentially win against his missed draws or smaller pairs without risking a loss to a better kicker or a trap.

I'm on the button in a 3-handed game with 8-10 offsuit. While this is a marginal hand, calling is justified here: (1) It's only 100 more to call, relatively cheap to see the flop, (2) I have superior position and can leverage that postflop, (3) In 3-handed play, my range from the button should be relatively wide, (4) 8-10 has decent playability potential on many flops - I can make straights, pairs, or flush draws depending on the board, (5) My stack size (10,255) allows me to play flexibly. I'll take a flop and use my positional advantage to dictate postflop action based on how the board develops.

I am holding Qs Tc in the small blind. The button, Claude, has limped into the pot. In a three-handed game, Qs Tc is a strong starting hand. I will raise to 400 to take initiative, punish the limp, and potentially win the pot immediately or narrow the field before the flop.

3-handed late in tournament. In BB with 98o facing BTN limp and SB raise to 400 (need 300 more). Pot 600, getting 2:1 direct odds plus implied odds with playable hand that can flop strong draws/straights/flushes. OOP but multiway pot likely if call, and stacks deep enough (80+BB). Flash aggressive raiser from SB, Claude loose limper. Calling exploits pot odds and position, planning to play postflop cautiously or aggressively if hit.

Calling 300 to play 8-10 offsuit from the button. Strong pot odds (4.5:1), position advantage, and decent playability. With 3-handed dynamics, I can leverage button position to dictate postflop action. Stack size (10,155) allows flexible play. The hand can make straights, pairs, and draws on many flops.

I have two overcards (Queen and Ten) on a low, connected board of 2-4-5. This board texture is generally better for the callers' ranges than for my pre-flop raising range. Being out of position against two opponents, a continuation bet is risky as it's unlikely to generate enough folds and I have very little made value. I will check to see how the field reacts and potentially realize some equity if the hand stays cheap.

Flop of 2d4s5s is extremely draw-heavy with flush and straight draws possible. My 98o completely whiffs: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot absent), no flush draw, just two overcards for ~12% equity at best vs random hands, less vs villain ranges. Flash (aggressive raiser pre) checks first in, suggesting possible weakness or trap, but multiway with Claude (loose) who limps/called wide. Pot 1200, deep stacks. Betting as bluff risks calls from draws/pairs and isolation issues OOP next; fold equity low 3-ways. Checking takes pot control, free river card, keeps in cheap, can bluff later streets if board bricks or rep strength if checked through. Consistent with preflop float/exploit plan but folding equity now postflop.

Betting 400 on the flop with 8♥ T♠ (open-ended straight draw) on a 2♦ 4♠ 5♠ board. Both opponents checked, indicating weakness and giving me an opportunity to apply pressure from the button. This bet accomplishes multiple goals: (1) Fold equity - take down the pot immediately with weak holdings, (2) Draw equity - I have 8 outs with solid drawing potential (~32% equity), (3) Position leverage - aggression from the button is standard and profitable, (4) Pot odds - I'm risking 400 to win 1,200, which is profitable given my combined fold/draw equity. Stack sizes support this play. Standard 1/3 pot sizing maintains flexibility for postflop play.

On a flop of 2d-4s-5s, my Qs Tc has very little equity. This board texture heavily favors the ranges of the players who called my pre-flop raise, as they are more likely to hold small pairs, suited connectors, or hands containing a 3 (making a straight). Claude's bet into two players, combined with Elon still to act behind me, makes a call or a bluff raise very risky. I am out of position and my overcards are not 'clean' outs given the straight and flush possibilities on the board. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Facing a 400 bet into a 1600 pot on a highly coordinated 2d4s5s flop (flush draw, wheel straight draws, open-enders like 36, 67). My 98o has zero equity: no pair, no straight draw (98 doesn't connect), no flush draw, just live overcards for weak gutshots at best (~10-15% equity vs wide range, less vs polarized donk bet range). Pre-flop call was speculative for multiway pot with position potential, but flop checked through to Claude (BTN limper-caller OOP) who donk bets after two checks, Flash folds. Claude's range: draws (spades, wheel), weak pairs (22-55? but unlikely), or bluffs/traps. Calling commits ~25% pot OOP with no equity or fold equity, poor implied odds (board likely worsens), reverse implied if behind. Raising as bluff/semi-bluff has fold equity but risks OOP shove/call from draws/strength into deep stacks (mine 200BB, his ~90BB), poor post-flop if called. Folding maximizes EV by avoiding dead money bleed in unplayable spot, preserves stack for better situations late tournament 3-handed. Consistent with pot control check pre-action.
I am on the button with QJ offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I will open for 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand with positional advantage.

King-seven offsuit is a marginal hand at best, and from the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (effective 2x over blinds), it's not worth defending out of position. Flash has been aggressive with frequent raises from late position throughout the history. My hand lacks playability post-flop OOP—dominated if facing a king, and weak otherwise. Raising has no fold equity with this holding, and calling bloats the pot OOP with a speculative hand on deep stacks. Preserve stack for better spots; folding maximizes EV long-term.

Raising with AQ to 700 total chips. AQ is a premium hand and should be played aggressively from the big blind against a button raise. This 3-bet builds the pot with a strong hand, defines my range, and gives me the best position to win the hand either preflop or postflop. The raise size (roughly 2.3x Flash's raise) is standard and not overcommitting given my stack size of 10955.

I opened for 300 with QJo on the button, and Claude in the Big Blind has put in a small 3-bet to 700. I need to call 400 more into a pot that will be 1450, giving me excellent pot odds (roughly 3.6 to 1). In a three-handed game, QJo is strong enough to defend against a small re-raise, especially since I will have positional advantage for the remainder of the hand. I will call to see a flop.

C-betting on the flop with AQ and a strong flush draw. The board (5s 2s Tc) is relatively dry and favors my 3-bet range. I have multiple ways to improve (9 flush outs, potential overcards) and this bet builds the pot while maintaining control. Sizing at 900 is roughly 60% of the 1,450 pot—standard c-bet sizing that isn't overcommitting but builds value. Flash's check gives me the initiative to bet aggressively.

I called a small 3-bet pre-flop with Queen-Jack offsuit to leverage my positional advantage. However, the flop of 10-5-2 with two spades missed my hand entirely. Claude is leading for 900 into a pot of 1,450 (roughly 62% pot), which is a strong bet following his pre-flop aggression. Given that I have no draws (no flush draw, no straight draw) and only two overcards, I lack the equity to continue profitably. Claude's range in a 3-bet pot often includes pocket pairs, strong Tens (like ATs/KTo), or flush draws. Even if he is on a draw, I am barely ahead, and if he has a made hand, I am drawing thin to only six outs. I will fold and preserve my stack.

4d2s is one of the worst possible starting hands: low, offsuit, disconnected, with minimal playability even in position. On the button, I have positional advantage, but calling 100 into a 150 pot offers terrible implied odds and equity against likely calling ranges from blinds who may defend wide or 3-bet. Raising would be a bluff with no post-flop potential. Folding preserves my deep stack (20k+) for premium spots in this late tournament stage (hand 129/150), maximizing EV long-term. Opponents' histories show aggressive play, but this spot isn't worth entering.

Heads-up against the Big Blind with A6o. I've already posted 50 in Small Blind. Calling 50 more to see the flop requires minimal additional investment (3:1 pot odds) and A6 has respectable equity in heads-up play. Folding surrenders the posted blind. Raising is too aggressive with this marginal hand. Call to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Claude has completed the Small Blind, indicating a range that is likely wide or potentially trapping. While an Ace is often ahead of a limping range, Trey is a weak kicker. I have the option to raise to 300-400 to take the pot immediately or define Claude's hand, but checking allows me to see a flop for free and keep the pot small with a marginal holding. Given the tournament stage and my current stack, I will take the free flop and evaluate post-flop.

On the flop with Ac6h on 3c8sTd, I have top pair with an Ace kicker. I'm out of position but Flash just checked, showing weakness. This is a good spot to bet for value. My Ace is likely the strongest hand in this heads-up flop, and I should build the pot while Flash has a wide range that includes many hands worse than my pair. A bet of 150 (75% of the 200 chip pot) is reasonable - it's not aggressive enough to scare off medium-strength hands I want to value, but it builds the pot against a range that likely contains many weak holdings. I'll take down the pot frequently here while building value when called.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a Ten-Eight-Trey rainbow board in a limped blind-vs-blind pot. Claude led for 150 into a 200 pot. In a battle of the blinds, my hand is strong enough to continue, as Claude could be leading with overcards, draws (like Jack-Nine or Nine-Seven), or air. A raise would likely only be called by better hands (a Ten or an Eight), while calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and see how the turn develops. I will call and reassess on the next street.

Turn check with top pair Ace-kicker on a paired board. The 8d is a dangerous card that likely improved Flash's range. Out of position with a marginal hand, I should exercise pot control and let Flash take the initiative. If Flash bets, I can make a fold/call decision with more information. If Flash checks back, I'm ahead and can evaluate the river situation. This conservative approach preserves my stack while maintaining flexibility for the river action.

On the turn, the board pairs the Eight, which is generally a neutral card. Claude, who led the flop, has now checked. This check often indicates a desire to reach showdown cheaply with a marginal hand or a realization that their flop lead didn't fold out my range. My pair of Treys with an Ace kicker has decent showdown value in a limped pot, especially against a Small Blind's wide range. There is little value in betting here, as better hands like a Ten or an Eight will not fold, and I'm ahead of most of his air or overcard hands. By checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I see the river for free.

River bet with top pair top kicker. Flash checked the turn on the 8d, indicating weakness or a medium pair at best. A value bet of 250 (50% of the pot) extracts value from weaker holdings while being reasonable against stronger hands. In heads-up play, my A6 is strong enough to bet for value when the opponent has shown weakness. This bet likely wins the pot immediately against missed draws while building the pot against worse pairs.

I am in a blind-vs-blind limped pot with bottom pair (3s) and an Ace kicker. Claude completed from the Small Blind, bet the flop, checked the turn, and is now leading for 250 into a 500 pot on the river. His line is somewhat polarized or represents thin value (like a weak Ten). By checking the turn, I've under-represented my hand, making it look like I have nothing or just Ace-high. In a blind-vs-blind battle, ranges are extremely wide, and a pair of 3s often has enough showdown value to call a half-pot bet. Given the pot odds (3:1), I only need to be right 25% of the time. I will call to see if my pair of 3s holds up against his missed overcards or a possible lower pair.

Raising from the button with 8-Js in 3-handed play. The small blind (Flash: 8,075) and big blind (Elon: 20,470) have committed money. My position, stack depth (113 BB), and hand's playability (potential straights) make this a standard aggression spot. Raising to 300 applies appropriate pressure while maintaining position if called. This leverages my button advantage and can win the pot outright or create a profitable post-flop situation where I act last.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 3x the big blind. While the game is three-handed and opening ranges are wider, T6o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop against an active opponent, and Elon (the chip leader) is still to act behind me. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

3-handed BB vs Button raise to 3x. J5s is suited connector with straight and flush potential, good postflop playability, and solid implied odds given deep stacks (effective ~11k). Button range is wide late position, so ~31% pot odds are favorable for defense. Folding is too tight in BB; raising is unnecessary bluff with playable hand. Call to realize equity multiway potential or heads-up.

Flop K98 rainbow, heads-up vs button raiser. J5s has backdoor flush draw and straight draw, but no made hand or strong draw. Overcard J gives some showdown vs bluffs, but villain's range hits this board well (Kx, 98, sets, draws). As first to act in BB with ~31bb effective stacks, donk betting is suboptimal; check allows pot control, realizes equity if checked behind, or floats small cbets. Folding pre was tight; now check-fold to likely cbet or check-call small for implied odds.

I have a pair of eights on a 9c-8d-Ks flop in a heads-up situation. Elon checked, indicating weakness or a medium-strength hand. With a solid pair, position, and chip advantage, betting 400 (roughly 60% pot) is standard. This bet wins some pots immediately, builds the pot when I have the best hand, and allows me to gather more information about Elon's holding while maintaining positional control. The bet size is appropriately aggressive without overcommitting.

Facing a 38% pot cbet on a dry K98 rainbow flop heads-up vs aggressive button raiser. J5s suited has only backdoor straight/flush potential and 3 outs to top pair (overcard J), for roughly 10-15% equity vs typical cbet range heavy on Kx, 98, sets, and some Ax/connected draws. Pot odds require ~28% equity to call profitably, ignoring future streets where villain likely continues with value. No fold equity on raise with marginal blocker. Deep effective stacks (10BB villain), but folding weak BB defense realizes no showdown value and preserves stack for premium spots late tournament (hand 130/150). Check-fold optimal per position and hand strength.

Holding 3h 2c on the button, which is statistically one of the weakest hands in poker. Although we are in a 3-handed game where ranges should be wider, this hand has almost no playability and very little equity against any calling range. With a healthy stack of over 80 big blinds, there is no reason to attempt a low-frequency steal or enter a pot with such poor cards. Folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play here.

Preflop in SB vs BB after Button folds. A3o is a strong enough hand to raise from the small blind in this spot with dead money in the pot (150 chips). Ace blocker reduces opponent's AA/KK likelihood, and we have initiative postflop. Raising standard size around 3x BB to build pot or take it down. Deep stacks allow flexibility postflop if called. Folding is too tight, limping invites multiway trouble unnecessarily.

Calling 200 into a 400 chip pot with 6-7 suited against Elon's 3x raise from the small blind. I'm getting 2:1 odds and have a playable hand with straight and flush draw potential. With deep stacks and postflop flexibility, this is a standard call in the big blind. I can easily get away from this hand if I miss the flop badly while maintaining my chip lead in the tournament.

Preflop raise from SB with A3o was standard to take initiative and build pot with decent blocker hand. Flop 4c5hJc completely misses: no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely). Ace-high has some showdown value but vulnerable to any Jx, pairs, or draws. Board is draw-heavy (clubs, OESD like 23/36/67/23s), favoring BB calling range. Deep stacks (20k+ effective vs Claude's 11k). Heads-up, first to act: betting as a bluff risks villain raising/playing back with equity while I have nothing to continue with; fold equity exists but sizing would need to be small (~200-300), and villain (Claude) has shown calling station tendencies in history. Better to check: pot control with marginal ace-high, realize equity when ahead if villain checks behind floats/weak hands, easy fold to bet. Avoid bloating pot OOP with air. Goal to maximize stack long-term favors discipline over spew.

Betting 300 into the 600-chip pot with 6-7 of hearts on a 4-5-J flop. I have an open-ended straight draw (14+ clean outs) plus a flush draw giving me excellent equity. Elon's check signals weakness, and I can profitably build the pot by betting roughly half the current pot size. This bet wins immediately against weaker hands or takes advantage of my strong draw equity if called.

Facing a 300 bet into 900 on a 4c5hJc flop OOP with As3d, which is pure air: ace-high no pair, no flush draw (spade ace blocks but irrelevant), no straight draw or gutshot. Board is highly coordinated with club flush draw, OESD potential (e.g., 2-3, 2-6, 6-7 suited), and Jx pairs crushing my range. Preflop I raised A3o for initiative and blocker value, but flop whiffed completely. Claude called preflop OOP and now leads into checked pot – strong range including Jx, sets, pairs 66+, flush draws, combo draws. Pot odds ~3:1 (300 to call into 1200 effective) but equity realization poor OOP; villain's history shows calling station vibes but also value betting strength (e.g., Hand 34, 35). Raising air OOP risks spew with no fold equity vs sticky opponent; check-fold maximizes EV long-term by avoiding bloated pots with nothing. Deep stacks but discipline preserves stack for better spots in late tournament.

3-handed on the button with K7o, an offsuit king with decent high card value. Button has a wide stealing range preflop, especially against blinds. K7o is strong enough to open raise for value/thin value and to steal dead money from blinds. Standard raise size around 3x BB (300) given typical sizing from history. Deep stacks allow postflop playability with position. Folding too tight, limping poor strategy.

5♦ 10♦ is a weak, speculative hand from the small blind. While I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 250 into 450), I'm out of position post-flop against Elon's button raise. The hand has limited equity against a raising range and no strong made hand value. With 250 chips to call on a relatively healthy stack, folding is the mathematically sound play. This preserves chips for stronger opportunities where I have better equity and position. With 19 hands remaining in the tournament, selective play remains important.

I am holding pocket eights (8d 8s) in the big blind. In a three-handed game, this is a premium hand, especially against an open raise from the button. Elon has been quite active, and his button opening range is likely very wide. While calling is an option to keep the pot manageable and trap, I prefer 3-betting for value and to take the initiative. A raise to 1,000 (roughly 3.3x the open) is a standard sizing to put pressure on his wider range and define the strength of his hand.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash in the big blind to 1000 after my button open to 300 with K7o. 3-handed, effective stacks around 7k with Flash. K7 offsuit is a marginal opening hand that plays poorly postflop: weak kicker, offsuit, limited equity vs a polarized 3-bet range (AA/KQ+/bluffs). Calling 700 into 1350 gives ~3.2:1 but postflop OOP for Flash, no, I'm IP as button. Still, dominated often, reverse implied odds if trailing. 4-betting risky as not strong enough, low fold equity vs aggressive Flash. History shows Flash aggressive but folding here preserves stack for premium spots in late tournament. Standard fold.

With 5-Qo from the button, I'm calling the 100 chip small blind. While this is a marginal hand in absolute terms, several factors make calling reasonable: (1) I have positional advantage as the dealer with last action, (2) the cost is minimal relative to my 11,905 stack, (3) with 133 hands played out of 150, I need to be selective but not overly tight, (4) both opponents have moderate stacks (Flash 8,375, Elon 19,570) which creates opportunities for post-flop play, and (5) 5-Q has some utility - it can make straights and I can leverage position to win unimproved hands. Folding would be unnecessarily tight from the button in this situation. I'll take the flop with positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with J3o. Claude has limped from the Button. While the price to call is only 50 chips more, J3o is an extremely weak hand to play out of position, especially with a chip leader like Elon in the Big Blind who could easily raise to squeeze. I prefer to wait for a better hand to commit chips.

Preflop in BB with 2d3s, a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against any limping range. Claude limped from button (wide range possible), SB folded, pot 250, and checking is free to realize equity or fold to flop aggression if needed. Raising trash for a small steal risks getting called or shoved on by better, unnecessary with deep stacks (effective ~11k) and goal to maximize stack long-term. Standard check to see flop.

Flop is Kd Ad Qc, a very coordinated and high board with possible straights (JQ/TJ), strong pairs (A/K/Q), and flush draws (diamonds). My 2d3s has no pair, no straight draw (gutshot to wheel unlikely), backdoor spade flush draw at best, but effectively air with 0% showdown value. Pot 250, deep stacks, first to act as BB. Betting would be a pure bluff with terrible blockers (no A/K/Q) and high likelihood of facing calls from Ax/Kx/draws. Claude's button limp range includes many Ax suited diamonds, broadway, suited connectors that hit this hard. Standard check-fold to preserve chips for better spots with 18 hands left. Goal is stack maximization, not spewing.

With pair of Queens on a K-A-Q flop after Elon checks, I should bet for value and information. The board is dangerous with two broadway cards, but my pair of Queens is strong if Elon doesn't have a premium holding. A 150-chip bet (60% pot) maintains good sizing, extracts value from medium hands, and keeps the pot manageable if I need to fold to aggression. This is a standard value bet on a strong but vulnerable hand.

Facing a 150 bet into a 400 pot on a Kd Ad Qc flop with 2d3s, which has zero equity: no pair, no straight draw (needs running low cards for wheel, improbable), weak backdoor diamond flush draw at best but blocked by my own 2d and no strong hand representation. Claude's button limp range is wide (suited broadway, Ax, suited connectors, diamonds) and hits this board hard with top pair (A/K/Q), two-pair combos, straight draws (TJ), flush draws. Calling commits 150 for poor implied odds given reverse implied odds if I hit unlikely draws, and deep stacks favor folding marginal spots to preserve stack for 17 hands remaining. Raising is a bluff with no blockers and fold equity diminished by Claude's wide range sticking around. Standard fold to maximize EV long-term.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives. In a three-handed game, pocket pairs are very strong starting hands. I will follow my standard opening procedure of raising to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or see a flop against one opponent.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB). Button range is wide due to position and player tendencies from history—Flash raises frequently but folds to aggression often. ATo offsuit is a strong 3-bet candidate: premium ace blocker, live ten, plays well postflop. Calling OOP risks multiway pots with BB behind and poor position. Folding too tight given pot odds (pot 450 to call 250). 3-bet sizing to 900 (3x the raise) applies pressure, folds out worse aces/broadway, builds pot with strong equity when called. Deep stacks (195BB effective) allow postflop flexibility if needed. Claude BB may call light but unlikely to 4-bet wide.

Folding 4♠J♦ against Elon's 3-bet. This is a weak, uncoordinated hand that doesn't have sufficient equity to play out of position against an aggressor. With only 16 hands remaining in the tournament, I should avoid marginal spots and preserve my stack for premium situations. The risk-reward is unfavorable here.

I have pocket fives on the button and opened for 300. Elon, the chip leader in the small blind, 3-bet to 900. In three-handed play, pocket pairs hold significant value. I am getting a good price to call (600 to call into a pot of 1300) and I will have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. 4-betting here would likely polarize the situation too much and put my stack at risk unnecessarily, whereas calling allows me to evaluate the flop and potentially hit a set or play a favorable low-card runout.

Flop of 6c-Jc-3c is a complete whiff for ATo offsuit: no pair, no flush draw (Ad blocker but irrelevant without suit), ace-high showdown value is weak on this monotone board favoring villain's suited calling range (e.g., AcXc, KcQc, JcXc, suited connectors). Preflop 3-bet got value/isolation but now OOP first to act into a pot of 1900 vs shorter stack (74BB). No fold equity for a bet/raise bluff given board texture hits button wide range hard. Check to realize equity (minimal) or induce bluffs/checks from cautious villain, then fold to aggression. Preserves stack for better spots in late tournament.

On this monotone flop of 6c Jc 3c, I have a pair of fives and a low club (5c). Elon, who 3-bet pre-flop, has checked to me. While I have the 5c, a 5-high flush is very vulnerable. Elon's range often contains higher clubs (Ac, Kc, Qc) or overpairs that he might be playing cautiously or trapping with. By checking behind, I exercise pot control, see a turn card for free, and avoid being check-raised off my modest equity. There is little value in betting here as I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call.

Turn pairs my Ten, giving me middle pair with top kicker (Aces) on a draw-heavy board (monotone clubs flop, now Td adds straight draw possibilities like 4-5 for 3-4-5-6-T). Preflop 3-bet range (Ax, broadway) hit villain's calling range decently; his flop check-behind suggests caution/missed draw rather than monster (sets, two-pair unlikely on monotone board without preflop strength). OOP, betting protects against clubs/chases, extracts value from weaker Ax/Jx/draws, denies equity to floats. Sizing 2/3 pot (1300 into 1900) balances value/protection; smaller risks free cards, larger overplays. Villain folds often to aggression per history. Checking risks giving free river to draws or facing check-raise.

On the turn, the board is 6c Jc 3c Td. Elon, who 3-bet pre-flop and checked the flop, has now led for 1,300 into a 1,900 pot. My pair of fives is very weak on this board, and while I have the 5c, it is the lowest possible flush draw. Given Elon's 3-betting range, any club he holds is likely higher than mine. Furthermore, with the J and T on board, many of his overcard combinations have now paired up or contain high clubs (Ac, Kc, Qc). Calling 1,300 represents a significant portion of my remaining stack with a hand that has very few clean outs and is likely dominated. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

3-handed on the button first to act preflop with Qh3c offsuit. Button opening range is very wide (45-60%+ of hands), and Q3o is comfortably in that range due to queen blocker, decent high card value, and position. Blinds are small relative to stacks (73BB and 119BB effective for them), so stealing adds dead money (150 in pot) with minimal risk. History shows frequent preflop folds and calls but button opens often succeed. Raising to standard 3x BB (300) builds pot OOP while maintaining fold equity. Limp/fold too passive; check-fold impossible first in.

7-3 is the worst starting hand in poker. Even with hearts providing a weak flush draw potential, this hand has minimal equity and no business being played from the small blind out of position against Elon's button raise. Folding preserves chips for higher-quality opportunities in the remaining hands of this tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 3s, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Elon, the chip leader, has raised from the button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 450), 43o plays very poorly post-flop and rarely hits anything strong enough to continue. With only 15 hands remaining in the tournament and being the shortest stack, I need to preserve my chips for better opportunities rather than defending with junk hands.

3-4 offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands with minimal equity and poor playability post-flop. Despite button position, this hand doesn't warrant a call. With 14 hands remaining and a healthy stack, selective play with premium hands is the correct strategy. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The Button (Claude) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Elon). While it's only 50 more to call, J6o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a deep-stacked opponent like Elon who has shown aggression. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 136 of 150), I prefer to fold this hand and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Ace-Seven offsuit. This is a standard opening hand from this position. Raising allows me to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage against the blinds. My stack is around 73 big blinds, and a standard 3x open has been effective throughout the tournament.

6c Js is a marginal offsuit hand OOP in SB vs BTN raise. Flash raises wide from button, but J6o lacks equity, blockers, or playability to justify calling 250 into 450 pot OOP. 3-betting is not justified without suited/better connectivity. Folding preserves stack late in tournament (hand 137/150) with deep effective stacks.

Folding 8s5c in the big blind. This is a weak holding with poor equity against Flash's button raise range. While I'm heads-up after Elon folded, my hand is too marginal to continue. With 13 hands remaining and a decent stack (11805), I should preserve chips for higher-value spots. Flash has 7025 chips remaining - if I want to pressure him, I should do so with stronger holdings. The cost-benefit of calling here with 8-5 offsuit doesn't justify the investment. Folding is the disciplined play.

3-handed on the button with deep stacks (207 BB effective). J5o is a marginal hand but playable for a steal raise from the button due to position advantage and fold equity against the blinds. Opponents from history (Flash aggressive but folds to pressure, Claude calls but not always strong). Standard open size 3x BB to 300 total. Builds pot if called, or takes it down pre-flop frequently to maximize stack in late tournament stages.

Holding A6s (Ace-Six of spades) in the small blind. Elon raises to 300 from the button with 20,420 chips. Pot is 450, and I need to call 250 with 11,755 chips. Pot odds are 1.8:1, which justifies a call with this hand. A6s has reasonable equity with ace-high potential, backdoor flush possibility, and decent playability. With 138 of 150 hands played, near tournament end makes stack preservation important, but proper pot odds dictate calling. Flash in the big blind is yet to act, and my hand can realize decent equity on many flops. Folding would concede the pot unnecessarily given favorable odds.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Spades and Four of Clubs, a very weak starting hand. Elon raised to 300 from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. Although the pot odds are somewhat attractive (200 to call into a pot of 700), this hand plays extremely poorly post-flop, especially when out of position against two opponents. Given that I am the shortest stack and we are approaching the final hands of the tournament, it is wiser to preserve my chips for a more advantageous situation rather than defending with a sub-par hand.

On the flop with As6s vs Elon, I'm facing 8dQs9s. I'm in position to act first post-flop in this heads-up matchup. My hand has strong equity with 8 outs to a flush (the 9 remaining spades minus duplicates), plus ace-high and potential backdoor straights. With a pot of 700 and stacks relatively deep for near tournament-end, this is an ideal semi-bluff spot. A bet of 400 (roughly 57% of pot) accomplishes multiple goals: (1) takes down the pot immediately against hands that whiff, (2) gets value from worse holdings like King-high or underpairs, (3) builds the pot when called with a strong draw. This sizing is proportional to my 11,505 stack and standard for this flop texture. My position advantage and drawing equity justify the bet here.

Flop of 8d Qs 9s is extremely draw-heavy with spade flush draw and multiple straight draws (e.g., JT, T7). Holding J5o (Jh 5d), I have jack-high with no pair, no straight draw (my J doesn't connect meaningfully), backdoor diamond flush at best but no immediate equity. Pre-flop raise was a steal from button that got called by SB Claude, who now leads for 400 into 1100 pot (~36% pot bet). Facing a bet from SB who defended wide, his range likely includes strong hands (QQ, 99, 88, Q9, Q8 sets/straights) and draws (spades, JT suited). My hand has negligible equity (<20% vs typical calling range) and poor implied odds. Position doesn't help enough to float profitably deep-stacked near end of tournament. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 204BB effective; calling commits to difficult turns/rivers with trash. Consistent with GTO: fold weak air to continuation from defender on wet board.

Calling with 8-10 offsuit on the button in 3-handed play. Good pot odds (150:100), position advantage, and the hand has decent playability postflop. Stack sizes are reasonable, and calling keeps me competitive without overextending with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 6d. Claude limped from the Button. I only need to call 50 more to see the flop, giving me 5:1 pot odds. While 56o is a weak hand, the price is too good to pass up for a hand with some connectivity. I will complete the bet and see a flop, keeping the pot small while out of position.

Hand #139 pre-flop in BB with Jd2h, a weak offsuit hand with little playability. Both opponents have limped (Claude on button called 100, Flash SB completed), pot is 300 with no further investment needed. Raising light OOP with trash is -EV due to poor post-flop prospects multiway and out of position. Checking keeps pot small, allows seeing flop cheaply if it improves (e.g., two-pair or straight draw potential, though unlikely), or folding to post-flop aggression without cost. Near tournament end (139/150), preserve stack for better spots; no need to bluff-raise into limpers with this holding. Opponent tendencies from history show frequent limping/checking, so cheap flop viable but hand too weak to speculate heavily.

I have completely missed this flop with 6-high. The board contains a King and a Seven, and I have no draws. Given I'm out of position in a small, limped pot, there is no reason to bluff or invest further chips. I will check and likely fold to any aggression.

On the flop of 2s Kd 7c, I pair my deuce for bottom pair with a jack kicker, a marginal holding OOP in a multiway pot. The board is dry with no obvious draws, favoring strong hands like Kx, 77, or sets. Flash checked from SB, indicating possible weakness, but betting out thin into two opponents risks being raised or called by better. Checking maintains pot control, realizes equity cheaply, and induces bluffs or check-behinds from limpers' weak ranges. With deep effective stacks (Flash shortest at 7275) and tournament nearing end, preserve stack for premium spots rather than overplay weak pair. If checked through, turn may improve or allow free river.

Both opponents checked to me on the flop with a relatively dry board (2s-Kd-7c). I hold 8-10 offsuit, which is a weak hand currently with only straight draw potential (any 9 completes my draw). While position is valuable in 3-handed play, my hand is too marginal to profitably bet here against two opponents. A check gives me a free card to improve on the turn while maintaining position. If I bet and get called, I'm in a difficult spot with a marginal holding. Checking allows me to reevaluate on the turn with better information. With 12k chips and 12 hands remaining, prudent chip management is appropriate with this weak holding.

The board has paired the seven, and since the flop was checked around, it's possible no one has a King. However, I still only have six-high with no draws. In a small limped pot, there's no merit in bluffing into two opponents when I have zero equity. I will check and hope to see a free river, but I am prepared to fold if anyone bets.

On the turn, the board is 2s Kd 7c 7d, pairing the sevens, which strengthens hands like 77 into trips, K7, or 7x draws into two-pair. I hold bottom pair (2s) with a jack kicker, a weak holding vulnerable to any king, seven, or better pair in a multiway pot OOP. Pre-flop limps from Flash (short stack SB) and Claude (button) suggest wide ranges including suited connectors, Ax, pocket pairs, but Flash's check-check indicates caution. No strong value or semi-bluff here; betting risks raises from better hands or floats, bloating pot OOP with marginal equity. Checking maintains pot control at 300, allows free river card (potential runner-runner help unlikely but possible), induces bluffs/check-behinds from weak ranges observed in opponents' histories (frequent checking/limping). With Flash short (7275) and tournament late (139/150), avoid overcommitting stack; preserve for premium spots post-bubble if any. Checking realizes equity safely multiway.

Holding 8-10 offsuit with a board of 2s-Kd-7c-7d. Both opponents checked the flop and turn, indicating weakness. My hand has limited showdown value and only 4 outs to improve (any 9 for a straight). In this multiway pot against a paired board, betting with this marginal holding is not profitable—I'll either get called by better hands or force a fold of worse hands that weren't calling anyway. Checking gives me a free river card while maintaining position. With 12,105 chips and 12 hands remaining, prudent chip preservation is important. If either opponent shows strength on the river, I can fold; if checked through, my hand may still win at showdown. Checking is clearly the best line here.

The board has double-paired (7s and 2s) with a King. My best hand is the board: Two Pair, Sevens and Deuces, with a King kicker.
