Since there has been no aggression on any street, it is likely that no one has a King, a Seven, or a Deuce. However, any player holding an Ace would beat the board's King kicker (making their hand 7-7-2-2-A). There is no reason to bet here as I can only be called by an Ace or a hand that splits with me. I will check and hope to split the pot.

River pairs the deuces, giving me trip 2s (222) with Jd kicker on a paired board (77 and 22). Strong value hand in a limped multiway pot that checked to river. Beats top pair Kx (common in limp/call ranges), unpaired hands, and some bluffs. Vulnerable to 7x (full house 77722), but limpers' wide ranges (observed frequent limping/checking/passive play in history) include many Kx, Ax, suited connectors that call a bet. Pot 300 tiny, stacks deep except Flash (7275 short). Value bet ~2/3 pot (200) gets value from worse while controlling pot OOP. Larger risks fold equity reducing value but Flash short may shove over. Small-med bet maximizes EV late tournament.

Folding with 8-10 high on a double-paired board (2s-Kd-7c-7d-2d). Elon raised to 200 on the river after checking flop and turn, indicating a value hand or strong bluff. With such a marginal holding (8-10 high has minimal equity), the pot odds of 3.5:1 don't justify calling. I need approximately 22% equity to profitably call, but against Elon's range I likely have far less. With 12 hands remaining and reasonable stack depth, folding preserves chips while avoiding a marginal/losing situation.

The board is 2s-Kd-7c-7d-2d, creating two pair (Sevens and Deuces) with a King kicker. I currently hold 6-high, so my hand is exactly the board: Two pair, Sevens and Deuces with a King kicker. Elon has bet 200 into a 300 pot (making it 500). In a limped pot where every street was checked until the river, Elon's bet is often a polarized attempt to win the pot, likely holding either an Ace for a better kicker or a complete air-ball. However, if he has an Ace (A7 or A2 are unlikely, but even Ax), he wins. If he has a King, Seven, or Deuce, he wins. Since I have 5-6, I am losing to any hand containing an Ace, 3, 4, 8, 9, 10, Jack, Queen, or a made hand. I am only beating or splitting with a very narrow range of hands (like 34, 45, 35). Calling 200 to win 500 when I am playing the board and lose to any card higher than a six (specifically an Ace) is not profitable. I will fold.

I am on the button with 3s 4c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (hand 140 of 150) and I am currently the shortest stack among the three remaining players, there is no reason to risk chips on such a poor hand. Strategic discipline suggests a fold here to preserve my stack for a better spot.

9d5s is a trash hand: offsuit, unconnected, low cards with poor post-flop playability, especially from small blind (worst position). Flash has folded, so heads-up vs Claude in BB, but calling 50 into 150 pot (3:1 odds) offers insufficient equity for this hand. No straight/flush potential worth chasing. Deep stack (20k+), 10 hands left: preserve chips for premium spots to maximize tournament stack. Standard fold.

3-handed on the button first to act preflop (UTG equivalent). Pot 150 (SB 50 + BB 100), to call 100 to limp. 9c3c is suited with straight potential and flush draw equity. Standard button opening range 3-handed includes 50-70% of hands, and 93s fits comfortably. Prefer raising over limping to take initiative, build pot OOP with marginal hand, and deny equity. Raise to standard 3x BB size of 300 given consistent sizing in prior hands. Deep stacks allow for postflop playability. Folding too tight, limping invites multiway pot OOP.

2-6 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. I'm in the small blind facing a raise from Elon, and Flash (big blind) hasn't acted yet. With only 12,105 chips in a 150-hand tournament, I need to preserve my stack for stronger hands. This hand has no playability, especially out of position. There's no reason to invest further chips here. Folding is the only correct action.

I am in the big blind with 8s3s. Elon, on the button, has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650 if I call. While 83 suited is a weak hand, the pot odds are roughly 2.25 to 1. However, playing out of position against Elon, who has a massive stack and has been aggressive, is difficult. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 141 of 150) and I have a relatively short stack (7175) compared to the others, I should avoid marginal spots where I am dominated. 83s is too weak to defend even with suited potential.

2s Jc is too weak to play profitably from the button. While positional advantage is valuable, it doesn't overcome the fundamental weakness of deuce-jack offsuit. This hand has poor equity against most ranges and bad kicker issues. Better to preserve chips for premium hands in the final 9 hands of the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 6h, a decent suited connector. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and Elon in the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and try to win the pot pre-flop or play the hand with the betting lead.

5d7s is a weak offsuit gapped hand with poor postflop playability out of position in the BB facing a standard SB steal raise from Flash. Pot odds are decent (400:200), but equity against Flash's likely wide SB opening range is insufficient long-term, especially OOP. With deep stacks and few hands left, better to fold trash and wait for stronger spots to maximize stack.

I am on the button with Qh Jd in a three-handed game. QJo is a strong hand to open-raise with from the button to steal the blinds or play in position. I have 72.75 big blinds, which is enough to play post-flop, though I am the shortest stack at the table. My standard raise size in this tournament has been 300, and I will continue with that sizing to maintain pressure on the blinds.

Pocket Queens is a premium hand, top of my range. Button raise from Flash is likely wide given position and history of aggression. 3-betting isolates, builds the pot, and applies pressure. Claude in BB may call wide or squeeze, but QQ performs well multiway. Standard 3-bet size ~3x the raise to 900 total. Deep stacks allow flexibility postflop if called. Folding or calling is suboptimal.

Folding 9-7 offsuit against a 3-bet in the big blind. The hand is too weak against Elon's strong range, I'm out of position postflop, and stack preservation is important this late in the tournament (143/150 hands). Clear fold situation.

I am on the button with Qh Jd and opened for a standard raise of 300. Elon, who is the chip leader, responded with a 3-bet to 900 from the small blind. While I have position, QJo is a marginal hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially from a big stack who has shown a tendency to apply pressure to my opens. QJo is frequently dominated by an opponent's 3-betting range (AQ, KQ, AJ, KJ) and does not play particularly well in bloated pots when facing aggression. With only a few hands left in the tournament and being the shortest stack, I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity rather than committing more chips with a hand that is likely behind.

3-handed on the button, first to act preflop in an unraised pot (standard position order). Hold 8d5d, a suited connector with playability, good implied odds deep-stacked in position. Button opens very wide 3-handed to steal blinds or build pot OOP. Standard raise size 3x BB to 300. Excellent position postflop if called. Blinds stacks: SB 120BB, BB 69BB, my 210BB. Maximizes EV by applying pressure.

Calling with pocket threes from the small blind. The pot odds (250 to win 450) are favorable for set mining with a small pair. Position postflop is excellent relative to Flash, and my stack allows me to see the flop safely. Elon's button raise is wide-range, so calling to set-mine is standard. Overcommitting by raising would be overplaying pocket threes and risks inflating the pot before I see the flop.

Holding Jack-Four offsuit in the Big Blind against a button raise and a small blind call. Although the pot odds are tempting (200 to call into a 700 pot), J4o is a bottom-tier hand that lacks playability and realization of equity in a multi-way, out-of-position scenario. Given we are at hand 144 of 150, I will opt to fold and maintain my stack for higher-equity situations.

I have flopped a strong hand - a set of threes (3h3c on a 2s-3d-Td board). The board is relatively dry with limited connectivity, and I'm heads-up against Elon after Flash folded pre-flop. However, I'm out of position, which is a significant disadvantage post-flop. Checking is the prudent approach here because: (1) it allows me to gather information about Elon's hand strength, (2) I can control pot size while determining whether I'm ahead or behind, (3) I retain flexibility to check-call, check-raise, or fold based on his action, and (4) the dry board means my set is likely still strong on future streets. Betting out of position with a medium-strength made hand risks overcommitting before I fully understand his range. By checking, I let Elon decide to bet with his wide button-raising range, and I can make a more informed decision on his action while maintaining good position control for the turn and river.

3-handed, button vs SB heads-up on a 2s3dTd flop after my preflop raise got one caller. Board has two diamonds, low connected cards, very draw-heavy. I hold 8d5d: nut flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity), no straight draw but live cards. Claude checked OOP, giving me initiative in position with 207BB effective. His calling range from SB vs button raise: wide, includes Ax suited, pairs 66+, broadway, suited connectors that hit draws here. Pure check risks giving free cards to his equity or letting him realize if he has it. Semi-bluff bet applies pressure, charges his draws/worse made hands (sets unlikely), builds pot with my strong equity for when flush hits. Bet sizing ~2/3 pot (450 into 700) balances value from worse (TPTK, sets/pairs drawing dead-ish) and fold equity from air/overpairs. Position allows control postflop. High EV spot to bet vs passive history (Claude calls a lot but folds to aggression often).

Raising with a strong made hand (set of threes) on a dry board. The board has limited connectivity and few possible draws. Building the pot while ahead is the key objective - extracting value from Elon's wide button range while charging any potential draws. A 3x raise to 1350 total is appropriate sizing: aggressive enough to apply pressure but not overcommitting given stack sizes. The set is strong enough to play for value on the flop rather than waiting for future streets where turn cards could diminish my advantage or slow Elon's betting.

Facing a check-raise on a draw-heavy 2s3dTd flop with the nut diamond flush draw (9 outs) and position vs SB. Pot 2500, call 900 more (total 1350 this street) offers ~3.8:1 odds, needing ~21% equity. Vs Claude's polarized check-raise range (sets like 22/33/TT ~10-12 combos, strong draws like Adx/Kdx/45s, TP like T9/T8 suited), equity ~30-35% (dominates draws, live vs sets). Position allows turn control: bet if diamond hits (huge value), check-fold/br if brick. Folding nitty given nut draw/live cards; raising commits stacks prematurely with SPR still ~6 after call, better to realize equity postflop vs calling-station history (Claude calls aggressively but checks weak turns often). Optimal to call, pot-control, exploit dynamics.

Betting 1700 on the turn with a set of threes. The 6c is a blank that doesn't complete major draws, and my hand remains very strong. Elon has shown commitment through the flop action, and this bet extracts value while maintaining good stack efficiency. The 50% pot sizing is standard for value-betting a made hand on a relatively dry board, allowing Elon to call with a wider range while keeping the pot manageable. Getting to the river is critical - my set is strong, and the blank turn supports aggressive play.

Turn action: Claude leads 1700 into 5100 pot (~33% pot) OOP after flop check-raise. My 8d5d holds 9x diamond flush draw (~19% to improve river), gutshot potential minimal, no pair. Pot odds: 1700 to win 6800 (~20.2% breakeven) align perfectly with equity. Vs Claude's value-heavy check-raise+donk range (sets 22/33/TT ~12 combos, T3/T2 two-pair low, strong diamond draws AdKd/Jd etc semi-bluffing flop now value-betting), ~28-35% equity (flush outs crush draws, live vs sets). Position allows river control: bomb diamonds for value (even 8-high flush often good vs range), check-fold/check-call bricks. Post-call SPR ~1.8:1 effective (Claude ~4k behind into 8.5k pot), low risk to realize equity. History: Claude sticky caller but folds aggression selectively; raising commits stacks prematurely vs monsters with thin FE (his range calls wide), folding nitty squanders odds/EV late tournament (hand 144/150, preserve upside). Call optimal: low variance, exploits pricing, maintains stack flexibility.

River value bet with full house (3s full of 6s). The 6d is a blank that doesn't significantly help Elon's likely holdings. He showed strength by calling through flop and turn, indicating a made hand like pocket pair (now two pair) or similar, which my full house crushes. Only hands 66 and 22 beat me - relatively unlikely from his button range. Bet 4000 (~59% pot) for value to extract chips from his two pair, trips, or weaker hands while maintaining good position control. This sizing looks natural and keeps him in the hand with a calling range.

River 6d completes the diamond flush and pairs the 6s, giving me the ten-high flush with an 8 kicker (Td-8d-6d-5d-3d). Claude's range from check-raising the draw-heavy flop (2s-3d-Td), betting turn OOP, and now leading river for 4000 (37% pot, nearly pot-committing his 4655 stack) is strongly polarized toward monsters (full houses like 22/66/33/TT over 66s, ~12-15 combos) and completed strong diamond flushes (Adx/Kdx/Jdx/9dx combos that check-raised flop as nut/semi-nut draws). Weak flushes (e.g., 7d4d) unlikely to take this aggressive line preflop call-check-raise-bet-bet. My flush is crushed by most flushes in range (any 9d+) and dead to full houses. Bluffs rare on triple-barrel after check-raise. Pot odds ~3.7:1 require 21% equity; vs value-heavy range, equity ~15-20% (few worse flushes, no bluffs). Folding preserves stack late (144/150, 3-handed, my deep stack 177BB), avoids spew, low variance play to maximize tournament life/EV.

With 2-6 suited on the button, even positional advantage cannot justify playing the worst hand in poker. In the late stages of the tournament (hand 145 of 150), chip preservation is critical. Folding garbage hands from any position is fundamental poker discipline. Wait for premium holdings to maximize value.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket eights (8s 8c). Since Claude has folded, it's just me and Elon (Big Blind). Pocket eights is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. I want to raise to build the pot and take the initiative. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to test Elon's range and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a controlled pot in position (though I'll be out of position post-flop, the strength of 88 justifies the raise).

JQ offsuit is a strong defending hand in the BB versus a SB open-raise, especially with Flash's history of frequent raising likely indicating a wide range. Excellent pot odds: calling 200 into a 400 pot (3:1). Hand has good playability for postflop with straight potential and top pair possibilities. 3-betting risks bloating the pot OOP against an aggressive opponent who may call or shove his 65BB stack; calling keeps the pot smaller and allows realizing equity multiway if needed, but heads-up likely. Folding too tight late with deep stacks. Proceed to see flop.

I hold pocket eights on a very coordinated board: Ace-high and monotone diamonds. I don't have a diamond in my hand, which significantly reduces my hand's strength and playability. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board hits many parts of the Big Blind's calling range (Aces, suited diamonds). I will check to see how Elon reacts. If he bets, I am likely folding as I have very little equity against a range that includes any Ace or a single high diamond.

Flop is 7d Ad 4d, giving me the Qd for a strong Queen-high flush draw (one of the best possible on this board) plus overcard equity with QJ. Board is extremely drawy with three diamonds to the Ace, potential gutshots, and pairs are vulnerable. Flash checked from SB after raising pre-flop (likely wide range including Ax, broadway, suited connectors). With position and pot odds irrelevant (checked to me), leading out denies equity to his draws/weak Ax, builds pot for when we hit flush/straight, and charges him. His stack is 6575 (~65BB), mine deep at 173k effective. Betting 2/3 pot (~400) balances value from worse (lower flush draws, pairs like 77/44/Ax no diamond) and protection. Checking risks giving free card to complete draws or allowing check-raise with monsters. Late tournament, exploit SB check as weakness.

On a monotone diamond board with an Ace, my pocket eights (with no diamond) are in a very tough spot. Elon's lead after my check indicates strength or at least a strong draw. Since I have no diamonds and there is an overcard (Ace), I have very little equity to continue. In a tournament nearing its end, preserving my remaining stack is more important than calling down with a marginal hand on a highly coordinated board.

I am on the button with Ace-four offsuit. In a three-handed game, any Ace is a strong opening candidate from the button to exert pressure and potentially win the blinds. I will put in a standard 3x open raise to 300 chips.

A♠3♣ offsuit from SB facing a button raise to 300 (to call 250 into 450 pot). This is a marginal ace-high hand with poor kicker and no suited/connectors value for implied odds or playability. Button ranges are wide, but A3o gets dominated frequently by Ax with better kickers or pairs. BB (Claude) still to act and can squeeze or call wide. Postflop SPR would be low (~3:1 if called), making it hard to realize equity. With only 5 hands left, preserve stack for premium spots rather than defend weakly. Standard fold.

2-6 offsuit is the worst hand in poker. I'm out of position in the big blind against a button raiser with a reasonable stack. While we're near the end of the tournament (only 4 hands left), stack survival isn't optimal when facing marginal spots. The pot odds of 2.25:1 don't justify playing this trash hand. A clear fold here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

Preflop on the button with 5s3c offsuit, one of the weakest possible hands lacking connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Pot is 150 with blinds posted and likely limped/checked to me (to call 100). Even in position with multiway potential, this hand has poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and is easily dominated. Opponents have deep stacks except Flash at ~66BB, but no reason to speculate with trash this late in the tournament (hand 147/150). Folding preserves stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV.

5-4 offsuit is bottom-tier hand strength. While heads-up ranges are wider, this hand lacks both showdown value and playability. With a healthy 15k stack vs Flash's shorter 6.6k, I can be selective. Only 3 hands remain in the tournament, so folding weak hands and waiting for stronger opportunities is strategically sound. Preserving chips with marginal holdings is prudent near tournament end.

A4o on the button in 3-handed play is a clear raising hand. Button position is crucial for aggression, and both opponents are relatively short-stacked, making this a good spot to apply pressure and pick up chips. A 2.5x BB raise is standard sizing from this position.

J7o is a weak hand to defend from the Small Blind against a Button raise, especially being out of position for the rest of the hand. With only three hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to commit chips with a marginal holding when I can wait for a better spot or simply protect my current stack.

Facing a button raise to 250 after SB folds, pot is 400, and I need to call 150 more from BB with Ts9s. This is a strong speculative hand with suited connectors, good playability, straight and flush potential. Vs button's wide raising range (likely 40-50% of hands), T9s has excellent equity (~40% vs random) and implied odds with deep stacks (my 17820, Claude 15055). Pot odds are ~2.7:1, easily met. Folding too tight late in blinds; 3-bet risks too much with non-premium hand and isolates poorly OOP. Standard play is to call and realize equity postflop OOP, where suited connectors perform well multiway but here heads-up likely.

Heads-up on the flop OOP with top pair (tens) and decent kicker (9), plus backdoor spade flush draw and straight potential (gutshot with 9 to 7-8-J). Board is Th-3s-5h: relatively dry but two hearts create flush draw outs that button's wide raising range (suited broadways, Ax, connectors) hits often, plus overcards and straight draws. Pot 550, deep effective stacks (~15k). As pre-flop caller OOP, standard to check first: induces bluffs/value bets from worse (e.g., weaker Tx, pairs, draws), allows check-raise strong hands or check-fold if villain shows strength. Betting risks folding out better equity realizations and vulnerable to check-raises. Late in tournament (148/150), preserve stack while realizing equity multi-street. Opponent Claude aggressive from history, likely c-bets wide.

Continuation bet on the flop with A4o after preflop aggression. Board is Th3s5h and Elon checked, indicating weakness. I'm in position with initiative. A 200 chip bet (approximately 1/3 pot) is standard sizing to show strength, take down the pot, or get valuable information if called. This bet maintains pressure while keeping my risk manageable with a marginal hand.

Facing a 200 bet into 750 pot (pot odds ~3.75:1) on Th3s5h flop OOP with Ts9s for top pair + 9 kicker, backdoor spade flush draw, and open-ended potential vs worse. Claude's button c-bet range is wide (Ax suited/hearts, weaker Tx like TJ/T8, pairs 66-44, draws like 67s/46s/24s, bluffs), which I beat much of (weaker pairs/draws fold, worse Tx call/raise light). Equity strong (~55-65% vs polarized c-bet range), implied odds deep (~17k stacks), reverse implied minimal with top pair. Folding top pair OOP too tight late tournament (148/150); raising risks bloating pot OOP vs aggressive Claude (history of value-heavy lines but wide pre), isolates bluffs unnecessarily. Call realizes equity cheaply, controls pot, keeps bluffs in, sets up turn check-raise/value bet if improves. Multiway history irrelevant (Flash folded), preserve stack for bubble/ICM but this spot +EV.

Turn board Th 3s 5h 7d OOP with Ts9s for top pair tens + 9 kicker, backdoor spade flush draw (3 spades total), and some straight potential in range interactions. Pot 950, deep effective stacks (~14k). Flop c-bet from Claude's wide button range (Ax, suited broadways/connectors, pairs/draws) polarized after my call; turn 7d is a blankish card that misses many draws (no flush complete, straights like 46/68 improve subtly but not directly), improving few strong hands (67s, 45s) while hitting bluffs/floaters (78x, J8x). Checking standard OOP with medium-strength hand: pot controls, induces bluffs/value from worse Tx (T8/J suited), draws (hearts like A4h/KJh, wheel draws), keeps aggression low late tournament (148/150, ICM relevant with short Flash but deep vs Claude). Betting risks check-raises from Ax/overpairs/sets, folds out equity from worse; thin value minimal vs aggro Claude who calls wide. Check sets up check-call/raise villain's turn bets (likely 50-70% freq), realizing equity multi-street while preserving stack near end. History shows Claude bets aggressively but folds to resistance; exploit post-check.

Elon has shown weakness by checking twice (flop and turn). My A4o is likely the best hand here against his range of weak holdings. The turn card (7d) didn't complete any obvious draw, and checking would allow him a free river card. I maintain position and have shown consistent aggression (preflop raise + flop bet), so continuing to apply pressure is the natural play. A 550 chip bet (~60% of the pot) is a balanced sizing that forces Elon to make a decision while still being reasonable given my hand strength. This bet maintains the narrative of my aggression while trying to take down the pot immediately or win on the river with the best hand.

Turn board Th 3s 5h 7d OOP with Ts9s: top pair tens + 9 kicker (beats most Tx in villain's wide button range like TJ/T8 suited, T7 possible but narrow), backdoor spade flush draw adds ~2-3% equity. Pot 1500, facing 550 bet (~27% pot, standard value/bluff size from Claude's aggressive history). Claude's range after flop c-bet/call polarized: value (sets unlikely on this board, two-pair T5/T3/57 rare, straights 46s/24s improved, better Tx/OTPs/overpairs); bluffs/draws (missed hearts like AhKh, floats like J8s/QJs that turn air, gutshots). Equity ~60%+ vs range (beats bluffs/weak value, lives vs better). Pot odds excellent (550 to win 2050 post-call ~2.7:1, crushes even if 33% equity needed). Deep effective stacks (~14k), implied odds good if improves (river spade/FD/straightens), reverse implied low with TP. Folding top pair OOP too tight late (hand 148/150, stacks healthy, no ICM pressure with short Flash out); raising OOP bloats pot vs aggro Claude (folds bluffs we want, gets snapped by value, awkward river OOP), check-raise value thin. Call exploits wide range, induces river bluffs/checks from worse, realizes equity cheaply multi-street, pot controls OOP. Consistent with prior lines: called flop, induces thinner value. History: Claude bets aggressively but overbets sometimes; call keeps pressure low near end, maximizes EV preserving stack.

River board Th 3s 5h 7d Jc OOP with Ts9s: top pair tens, 9 kicker (strong vs villain's wide button range including weaker Tx like T8s/T7s/T6s, lower pairs, missed draws like hearts/Ax/spades floats, backdoor spade equity didn't hit). Turn 7d completed possible 3-4-5-6-7 straights in Claude's range (e.g., 46s/26s/64s suited connectors he raises wide), Jc adds JT/Jc two pair, QJ/KJ/AJ straights unlikely but possible (8-9-T-J-Q no 8/9/Q; T-9-8-7-J possible if he had 89 suited). Claude's double-barrel line (flop c-bet 27% pot, turn 550 into 950 ~58%) polarized: value (sets rare Th/55/33, two-pair 53/T5 rare, straights 46x, better T like TA/TQ, OTPs); bluffs (missed flush draws Ahxh/Kxh, overcards AQo/KQo floats, pure air Jx/8x). Equity ~50-60% vs range (beats bluffs/weak Tx/draws, loses to straights/better). Pot 2050, deep stacks (16k/14k effective, Flash short folded irrelevant), hand 148/150 ICM mild (pay jumps but deep preserve stack). OOP river first to act: betting thin value (size 800-1200 gets raised by better Tx/straights/sets, called by worse pairs/draws we beat but extract less OOP); risks bloating final pot awkwardly. Checking optimal: pot controls, induces bluffs/checks-behind from worse Tx/pairs/draws (Claude history shows check-backs e.g. hands 15/22/39), allows hero-call vs value bets (excellent pot odds late, consistent check-call line from flop/turn signals medium strength he exploits lightly), realizes equity cheaply. Raising impossible (no bet). Folding/check absurd with TP. Check exploits aggressive Claude (bets wide but overfolds to resistance history e.g. 11/18/35), sets up +EV call/ fold riverside, maximizes stack EV near end.

River check with A4o. While I've shown aggression throughout this hand (preflop raise + flop/turn bets), the river J is a problematic card that likely completes Elon's range. My A4o is weak and likely loses to any pair he might hold. Elon's river check suggests either weakness or a moderate hand. Betting here would only get called by better hands or force a fold from weaker holdings I can beat. By checking, I preserve chips and can see the outcome of this hand without further risk. Given we're near the end of the tournament (hand 148/150), this is a sound approach with a marginal holding.

I am on the button with a decent suited hand (Jc7c) in a three-handed game. Since this is the penultimate hand of the tournament (Hand 149 of 150) and I am currently the shortest stack among the three, it is important to play aggressively from the button to pick up blinds or play a pot in position. A standard 3x open is appropriate here to put pressure on Elon and Claude.

85h is a marginal suited connector, but very weak for playing out of position in the small blind against a button raise from Flash, who is frequently aggressive preflop. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 effective (250 to call into 700 total pot after call), but postflop OOP is difficult with this hand. Equity heads-up vs loose button range ~35%, but multiway risk if BB defends, and no initiative. Near end of tournament (hand 149/150), chip leader with 189bb stack; preserve for better spots rather than speculative play. Folding is standard and disciplined.

Calling 200 more into 450 pot with 2.25:1 odds. 5♠4♠ has approximately 30-40% equity against Flash's wide button-raising range (20-30% of hands). The pot odds justify the call despite being out of position. Minimal stack risk relative to chip total. The hand has reasonable postflop potential with flush draw and wheel straight possibilities.

Betting 400 on the flop with a strong semi-bluff. I have 12+ outs (wheel + straight + pair outs) against Flash's range and his check indicates weakness. With ~35-40% equity, this bet gets value from my draw, applies pressure, and wins the pot immediately against many hands. The bet size is appropriately sized at roughly half-pot to Flash's shorter stack (6,425 chips), making it harder for him to continue without strength. This is a +EV play maximizing my chances in the final hand of the tournament.

Pre-flop, I raised from the button with Jc7c and was called by Claude in the big blind. The flop comes 3d-9h-4d, which misses my range significantly. Claude has led out with a 'donk bet' of 400 into the 650 pot. Given that I have only Jack-high with no direct draws (only a backdoor club draw) and am currently the shortest stack, there is little incentive to contest this pot. Claude's lead often indicates a piece of the board (like a 9 or a small pair) or a diamond draw. With only one hand remaining after this, preserving my remaining 6,125 chips is more prudent than floating or bluff-raising against a chip-leading opponent who has shown recent aggression.

3-handed on the button with 78o, a playable connector with good postflop potential. Facing a small bet of 100 into a 150 pot (pot odds ~2.5:1 direct, but position crucial). SB (Claude) likely raised small, BB (Flash) called. Deep effective stacks (mine 189BB, Claude 146BB, Flash 63BB). Optimal to isolate raise from button, leveraging position to play profitably postflop or take it down pre. Raising to 300 total (3x the facing bet) builds pot while maintaining fold equity vs weak ranges. Calling is fine but raising extracts value/thin value from worse. Folding too tight for last hand maximization in position. Last hand, but play +EV.

Folding K3o in the small blind. This is a weak hand with poor equity, especially out of position against Elon's button raise. With a solid stack (14,605) and only this final hand remaining, there's no value in gambling with marginal holdings. Preserving chips with a weak hand is the correct strategic play.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Elon, the chip leader, has opened for 300 from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a pot that will be 650), Jack-Five offsuit performs poorly out of position and has very little playability. To maximize my final stack and follow professional standards, I will fold this hand rather than risk chips on a sub-optimal defense.
